title: Java Memory Model
summary: The formal specification of how threads interact through memory — visibility, ordering guarantees, and safe publication.

sections.content:
## What is the Java Memory Model?

The Java Memory Model (JMM), defined in the Java Language Specification, specifies the **minimum guarantees** the JVM must make about when writes by one thread become visible to reads by another. Without it, CPUs and compilers would freely reorder instructions, making concurrent code unpredictable.

The JMM is defined in terms of **happens-before** relationships — a partial ordering on actions.

sections.title: JMM — Thread Local Working Memory vs Main Memory
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Thread 1
        WM1[Working Memory\nlocal cached copies]
    end
    subgraph Thread 2
        WM2[Working Memory\nlocal cached copies]
    end
    MM[Main Memory\nShared State]
    WM1 <-->|read/write| MM
    WM2 <-->|read/write| MM
    style MM fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style WM1 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style WM2 fill:#d97706,color:#fff

sections.title: final fields have special JMM guarantees
sections.content: The JMM guarantees that final fields are visible to all threads after the constructor completes, without any synchronization. This is why immutable objects are always safely publishable — store once, read anywhere.

sections.title: Double-Checked Locking — Broken vs Fixed
sections.content:
graph TD
    subgraph Broken DCL
        B1["if (instance == null)"] --> B2[synchronized block]
        B2 --> B3["if (instance == null)"]
        B3 --> B4["instance = new Singleton()"]
        B4 --> B5["⚠️ Another thread may see\npartially constructed object!"]
    end
    subgraph Fixed DCL with volatile
        F1["private volatile static Singleton instance"] --> F2["if (instance == null)"]
        F2 --> F3[synchronized block]
        F3 --> F4["if (instance == null)"]
        F4 --> F5["instance = new Singleton()"]
        F5 --> F6["✓ volatile write flushes\nall writes before it"]
    end
    style B5 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style F6 fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.question: Why is double-checked locking unsafe without volatile in Java?
options: Because synchronized blocks are too slow without volatile
Because the JVM may reorder the write to the reference before the object is fully constructed
Because null checks are not thread-safe
Because static fields require volatile to be initialized
sections.explanation: Without volatile, the JVM may reorder the write to the instance reference before completing the object's constructor writes. Another thread doing the outer null check may see a non-null but partially initialized object. volatile's happens-before guarantees prevent this reordering.
